fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small clock display utility for the home screen widget. Basically users have been complaining that the time shown on different screens looks inconsistent — sometimes it's single digit hours with no padding, sometimes things jump around. It's a mess and it's all because every screen is doing its own thing.

What we need is one central place that handles the time formatting so everything looks the same. It should support the two display styles our users care about — you know, the standard full-day format and the more familiar half-day style (similar to what we did in the settings panel, refer to that folding logic). The half-day one is a bit tricky around noon and midnight so make sure those edge cases are handled correctly.

The thing needs to be callable from a test harness that feeds it JSON over stdin and prints results to stdout. Errors should come back in a clean, predictable way — not raw crash text that leaks implementation details.

Also important: keep the actual formatting brain separate from the input/output plumbing. We had issues last time where rendering logic got tangled with HTTP stuff and it was a nightmare to test. Don't repeat that.

Output should have the rendered time and also echo back which display style was used so callers can confirm what path ran. Test runner should save raw output files per case so we can diff them easily.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: render_time(hour: int, minute: int, mode: str) -> str

trigger_keywords: what does the output look like
what should be printed to stdout
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter must print exactly two lines to stdout: first a line in the form `time=HH:MM` containing the rendered clock string, then a line in the form `mode=<mode>` echoing the resolved rendering mode (e.g. `24h` or `12h`). There must be a trailing newline after each line. No extra lines, labels, or metadata should appear in the output.

In 12-hour mode, the folding rule is purely numeric: if the hour is 12 or greater (i.e., noon through 23), subtract 12 from it before rendering. If the hour is less than 12 (0 through 11), leave it unchanged. This means noon (hour=12) renders as `00`, hour=23 renders as `11`, and hour=9 renders as `09`. No AM/PM marker is appended — it is a pure numeric fold only.

trigger_keywords: how are single digit values shown
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Both the hour and the minute must always be zero-padded to exactly two digits. A single-digit hour like 9 must render as `09`, and a single-digit minute like 4 must render as `04`. This applies in both 24-hour and 12-hour modes. The separator between hour and minute is always a single colon, producing the fixed-width format `HH:MM`.

Malformed or incomplete commands must be reported as normalized, language-neutral error category strings — for example, a message indicating a missing required field or an unknown mode value. The adapter must never let raw host-language runtime exceptions or stack traces reach stdout. The error output should be predictable and human-readable without leaking implementation internals.

The test harness writes one file per case to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt`. For example, the first case (index 0) in `feature1_clock_24_hour.json` run with `--cases-dir public_test_cases` produces `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_clock_24_hour@000.txt`. Each file contains only the raw stdout from the program under test — no PASS/FAIL annotations. Output is namespaced by `<cases-dir>` so different case directories never overwrite each other. The default cases directory is `test_cases`.

trigger_keywords: should the renderer know about stdin
how to separate IO from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The pure rendering rule (taking an hour, minute, and mode and returning a formatted string) must live in its own logical unit with no knowledge of JSON, stdin, or stdout. A separate adapter layer handles all input parsing, mode resolution, calls to the renderer, and output printing. The adapter depends on the renderer's interface, not the other way around. This keeps the core testable in isolation and prevents I/O concerns from bleeding into formatting logic.

In 24-hour mode the hour value is presented exactly as provided (0 through 23) with no folding or subtraction applied. Both hour and minute are zero-padded to two digits and joined with a colon. For example, hour=23 minute=4 renders as `23:04`, and hour=9 minute=54 renders as `09:54`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: test cases for feature1 clock 24 hour
show me feature1 clock 24 hour test cases
what are the feature1 clock 24 hour test inputs
give me the feature1_clock_24_hour test file
what does feature1 clock 24 hour expect
test cases for feature2 clock 12 hour
show me feature2 clock 12 hour test cases
what are the feature2 clock 12 hour test inputs
give me the feature2_clock_12_hour test file
what does feature2 clock 12 hour expect

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to what we did in the settings panel, refer to that folding logic
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 12-hour folding rule defined in Feature 2: subtract 12 from the hour if it is >= 12, leave unchanged if < 12. This is a pure numeric operation with no AM/PM suffix, as specified in the PRD's Feature 2 section and validated by feature2_clock_12_hour.json test cases.

context_pointers.vague_reference: We had issues last time where rendering logic got tangled with HTTP stuff
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The architectural constraint requiring strict separation between the core renderer (pure function: hour + minute + mode → string) and the I/O adapter (JSON stdin parsing + stdout printing), as described in the Architecture & Engineering Constraints sections on SRP and DIP.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect of the requirement. Please check the existing codebase for any related patterns, and feel free to ask me a more specific question about the output format, the hour-folding behavior, padding rules, error handling, or the test harness file structure.

feature1_clock_24_hour.json.description: Render an instant as a 24-hour wall-clock string. The adapter receives the hour-of-day (0-23) and minute together with the rendering mode '24h'. The hour is shown as-is and both hour and minute are zero-padded to two digits, joined by a colon. The output echoes the resolved mode so a caller can confirm which rendering path was taken.

feature2_clock_12_hour.json.description: Render an instant as a 12-hour wall-clock string. The adapter receives the hour-of-day (0-23) and minute together with the rendering mode '12h'. Any hour at or after noon has 12 subtracted from it before rendering, while hours before noon are shown unchanged; both hour and minute are zero-padded to two digits and joined by a colon. The output echoes the resolved mode.
